steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for the main idea of the passage. Hawthorne's central point is that historical knowledge is like a map — accurate but cold — leaving historical figures as 'mere names' rather than vivid people.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'historians should write about little-known people as well as famous people': the passage never argues for broadening the subject matter of history. ✗
  • Choice B — 'historical figures are usually documented as a collection of facts, rather than as a representation of their personalities': directly captures the map-versus-landscape-painting contrast and the idea that figures remain 'mere names.' ✓
  • Choice C — 'historians should record fictional stories told about famous figures': misreads the fireside-legend reference; Hawthorne observes legends make figures vivid but does not prescribe recording fiction. ✗
  • Choice D — 'paintings may be more beautiful than maps, but maps are more useful': reverses Hawthorne's point; he values the charm of landscape painting over the cold utility of maps. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
